questions.title: Question 1
questions.question: An analyst uses Python to programmatically query Databricks SQL and retrieve results as a DataFrame. Which connection method is most appropriate for this use case?

choices.A: ODBC driver — best for interactive desktop BI tools
choices.B: JDBC driver — best for Java-based applications
choices.C: Databricks SQL Connector for Python (`databricks-sql-connector`) via the Python driver
choices.D: Delta Sharing — enables programmatic data access

questions.shortAnswer:
The Databricks SQL Connector for Python (`databricks-sql-connector`) is the recommended driver
for Python applications querying Databricks SQL. It uses the Thrift protocol over HTTPS and
returns results as Python data structures compatible with pandas. ODBC and JDBC are typically
used by BI tools and JVM-based apps, not Python scripts.

questions.title: Question 2
questions.question: A Pro SQL warehouse is configured with `max clusters = 3`. When does a third cluster spin up?

choices.A: When the first query is submitted
choices.B: When both existing clusters are fully utilized and new queries are waiting in queue
choices.C: When the warehouse has been running for more than 1 hour
choices.D: When any query takes longer than 60 seconds

questions.shortAnswer:
Multi-cluster scaling in Pro warehouses is demand-driven. A new cluster spins up only when
existing clusters are at capacity and queries are queuing. With `max clusters = 3`, the third
cluster starts when both the first and second clusters are fully loaded. Clusters scale back
down when demand decreases.

questions.title: Question 3
questions.question: What does Photon acceleration do in Databricks SQL?

choices.A: Compresses Delta table files to reduce storage costs
choices.B: Replaces the JVM-based Spark SQL engine with a native C++ vectorized execution engine for faster query performance
choices.C: Caches query results in memory for instant replay
choices.D: Accelerates only Python notebook cells, not SQL queries

questions.shortAnswer:
Photon is Databricks' native C++ vectorized query engine that replaces parts of the JVM-based
Spark execution engine. It is optimized for analytical SQL workloads and significantly improves
performance for scans, aggregations, joins, and sort operations on large datasets. Photon is
enabled by default on Serverless and Pro warehouses.

questions.title: Question 4
questions.question: What does Databricks SQL query federation enable?

choices.A: Running the same query on multiple SQL warehouses simultaneously
choices.B: Querying external data sources (e.g., MySQL, PostgreSQL) directly from Databricks SQL without copying data
choices.C: Federating access control between multiple Unity Catalog metastores
choices.D: Sharing queries between different Databricks workspaces

questions.shortAnswer:
Query federation allows Databricks SQL to query external databases (MySQL, PostgreSQL,
Snowflake, etc.) directly via a connection object defined in Unity Catalog. Data is not
copied into Databricks — queries push down to the source where possible and retrieve
results on demand.

questions.title: Question 5
questions.question: A Serverless SQL warehouse is idle with no queries running. Does it incur compute charges during this idle period?

choices.A: Yes — Serverless warehouses charge continuously once started
choices.B: No — Serverless warehouses auto-stop when idle and billing pauses; charges resume only when queries run
choices.C: Yes — Serverless warehouses charge a reduced standby rate while idle
choices.D: No — Serverless warehouses are always free during idle periods regardless of configuration

questions.shortAnswer:
Serverless SQL warehouses auto-stop when idle (default 10 minutes). Once stopped, no compute
charges accrue. Billing resumes only when the next query arrives and the warehouse wakes up.
This pay-per-use model is a primary cost advantage of Serverless over Classic/Pro warehouses,
which charge for the full uptime even when idle.

questions.title: Question 6
questions.question: Which Databricks workspace role is required to create a new SQL warehouse?

choices.A: Any authenticated user can create a SQL warehouse
choices.B: Users with the "Can Use" permission on an existing warehouse
choices.C: Workspace Admin or users explicitly granted the "Create SQL warehouse" entitlement
choices.D: Only the account admin can create SQL warehouses

questions.shortAnswer:
Creating a SQL warehouse requires either Workspace Admin privileges or the "Create SQL
warehouse" entitlement explicitly granted to the user by an admin. Regular workspace users
can use existing warehouses they have permission on, but cannot create new ones without
this entitlement.

questions.title: Question 7
questions.question: What is the difference between Query Profile and `EXPLAIN` in Databricks SQL?

choices.A: They are identical — both show the query execution plan
choices.B: `EXPLAIN` shows the logical/physical query plan before execution; Query Profile shows actual runtime metrics (rows processed, time per stage) after execution
choices.C: Query Profile works only for streaming queries; `EXPLAIN` works for batch only
choices.D: `EXPLAIN` requires admin privileges; Query Profile is available to all users

questions.shortAnswer:
`EXPLAIN` outputs the query plan (logical, analyzed, optimized, or physical) before the query
runs — it is a planning-time tool. Query Profile (available in Query History) shows actual
runtime metrics after execution: rows read per node, execution time per stage, bytes shuffled,
and skew information. Both are useful for optimization but serve different purposes.

questions.title: Question 8
questions.question: How long are query results cached in Databricks SQL by default?

choices.A: 5 minutes
choices.B: 1 hour
choices.C: 24 hours
choices.D: Until the SQL warehouse is restarted

questions.shortAnswer:
Databricks SQL caches query results for up to 24 hours by default. The cache is invalidated
earlier if the underlying Delta table is written to (a new transaction is logged). This ensures
that cached results are never served for tables with new data, regardless of the TTL.

questions.title: Question 9
questions.question: A SQL warehouse is configured with auto-stop set to 30 minutes. A long-running query has been executing for 25 minutes. What happens at the 30-minute mark?

choices.A: The warehouse stops and the query is terminated
choices.B: The auto-stop timer does not apply to active queries — the warehouse stops only after the query completes and the idle period begins
choices.C: The query is paused and resumed when the warehouse restarts
choices.D: The warehouse sends a warning but continues running for another 30 minutes

questions.shortAnswer:
Auto-stop only triggers when the warehouse is idle — no active queries running. If a query is
in progress, the idle timer resets. The warehouse will not stop while queries are executing,
regardless of the configured auto-stop duration.

questions.title: Question 10
questions.question: What is a key difference between Databricks SQL and running SQL in a Databricks notebook?

choices.A: Notebooks support Delta tables; Databricks SQL does not
choices.B: Databricks SQL provides a purpose-built interface with query history, dashboards, alerts, and scheduled queries — notebooks require custom code for these capabilities
choices.C: Notebooks use the Unity Catalog; Databricks SQL uses only the Hive metastore
choices.D: Databricks SQL only supports SELECT statements; notebooks support all SQL commands

questions.shortAnswer:
Databricks SQL is a purpose-built analytics interface with built-in query history, result
caching, dashboarding, alerting, and scheduled query execution. SQL in notebooks requires
custom code or Jobs to replicate these capabilities. Both interfaces support the same
underlying Delta tables and Unity Catalog.

questions.title: Question 11
questions.question: Which SQL command lists all tables visible in the current schema within Unity Catalog?

choices.A: `LIST TABLES`
choices.B: `SHOW TABLES`
choices.C: `DESCRIBE SCHEMA`
choices.D: `SELECT * FROM information_schema.tables LIMIT 10`

questions.shortAnswer:
`SHOW TABLES` (optionally `SHOW TABLES IN schema_name`) lists all tables and views in the
current or specified schema. `DESCRIBE SCHEMA` shows schema metadata, not its table contents.
`INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLES` also works but requires a full SELECT query.

questions.title: Question 12
questions.question: A data engineer wants to grant a user the ability to read only the `email` column in a table, not the `ssn` column. Which Unity Catalog feature enables this?

choices.A: Row-level security policy
choices.B: Column-level permissions via `GRANT SELECT (column_name) ON TABLE ...`
choices.C: Table clone with only the allowed columns
choices.D: A view that selects only `email`, with SELECT granted on the view

questions.shortAnswer:
Unity Catalog supports column-level permissions: `GRANT SELECT (email) ON TABLE t TO user`.
This restricts the user to only the specified columns. Selecting `ssn` in a query will fail
with a permission denied error. Creating a view (Option D) also works but requires maintaining
an additional object.

questions.title: Question 13
questions.question: Which SQL syntax queries a Delta table as it existed at a specific version?

choices.A: `SELECT * FROM table AT VERSION = 5`
choices.B: `SELECT * FROM table VERSION AS OF 5`
choices.C: `SELECT * FROM table WHERE _version = 5`
choices.D: `SELECT * FROM table SNAPSHOT VERSION 5`

questions.shortAnswer:
`VERSION AS OF <n>` is the Delta Lake time travel syntax for querying a specific version.
The equivalent timestamp syntax is `TIMESTAMP AS OF '<timestamp>'`. Both are standard
Delta Lake time travel expressions supported in Databricks SQL.

questions.title: Question 14
questions.question: In Unity Catalog, what does `GRANT USE SCHEMA ON SCHEMA catalog.schema TO user` allow the user to do?

choices.A: Create new tables in the schema
choices.B: Resolve and access objects within the schema — required before any table-level privilege takes effect
choices.C: Delete the schema and its contents
choices.D: Grant other users access to the schema

questions.shortAnswer:
`USE SCHEMA` is a prerequisite privilege in Unity Catalog. Without it, a user cannot resolve
any objects within the schema even if they have SELECT on individual tables. `USE CATALOG`
on the parent catalog is also required. These privileges form a hierarchy: catalog → schema
→ table.

questions.title: Question 15
questions.question: A schema named `analytics` does not yet exist. Which SQL statement creates it only if it is absent, without raising an error?

choices.A: `CREATE SCHEMA analytics IGNORE IF EXISTS`
choices.B: `CREATE SCHEMA IF NOT EXISTS analytics`
choices.C: `CREATE OR REPLACE SCHEMA analytics`
choices.D: `UPSERT SCHEMA analytics`

questions.shortAnswer:
`CREATE SCHEMA IF NOT EXISTS schema_name` creates the schema only if it does not already
exist. Without `IF NOT EXISTS`, the command raises an error if the schema already exists.
`CREATE OR REPLACE` is valid for views and functions, not schemas.

questions.title: Question 16
questions.question: What is the difference between a `SHALLOW CLONE` and a `DEEP CLONE` of a Delta table?

choices.A: Shallow clone copies all data files; deep clone copies only metadata
choices.B: Shallow clone copies only metadata and references the source data files; deep clone copies both metadata and all data files
choices.C: They are identical — both copy all data and metadata
choices.D: Shallow clone supports time travel; deep clone does not

questions.shortAnswer:
`SHALLOW CLONE` creates a new table with its own transaction log but references the original
data files — no data is physically copied. It is fast and storage-efficient but depends on the
source files remaining available. `DEEP CLONE` copies all data files and the transaction log,
creating a fully independent copy suitable for backup or migration.

questions.title: Question 17
questions.question: When is `CONVERT TO DELTA` needed?

choices.A: When upgrading from Delta Lake version 1 to version 3
choices.B: When converting an existing Parquet table (or Parquet-based Hive table) to Delta format
choices.C: When migrating from Hive metastore to Unity Catalog
choices.D: When enabling Photon on a Delta table

questions.shortAnswer:
`CONVERT TO DELTA parquet.'/path/to/table'` converts an existing Parquet table to Delta
format in-place — it adds the Delta transaction log (`_delta_log`) without rewriting data
files. This is the standard migration path for teams moving from plain Parquet to Delta Lake.

questions.title: Question 18
questions.question: What does "attaching a workspace to a Unity Catalog metastore" mean?

choices.A: Connecting the workspace's network to the metastore's VNet
choices.B: Linking the workspace so that it uses the specified metastore for all catalog, schema, and table metadata — enabling Unity Catalog governance
choices.C: Granting the workspace admin access to all catalogs in the metastore
choices.D: Copying the workspace's existing Hive metastore tables into Unity Catalog

questions.shortAnswer:
A Unity Catalog metastore is a regional metadata store. Attaching a workspace to a metastore
means the workspace uses that metastore for all Unity Catalog objects. Multiple workspaces in
the same region can share a single metastore, enabling cross-workspace data governance and
sharing through a unified catalog.

questions.title: Question 19
questions.question: What information is available in `INFORMATION_SCHEMA.COLUMNS` for a Unity Catalog schema?

choices.A: The current row count for each table
choices.B: Column names, data types, nullability, and ordinal position for all tables in the schema
choices.C: The access history for each column
choices.D: Column-level statistics used by the query optimizer

questions.shortAnswer:
`INFORMATION_SCHEMA.COLUMNS` is a standard SQL information schema view that lists all columns
across tables in the schema, including column name, data type, ordinal position, and whether
the column is nullable. It is the standard way to programmatically inspect table schemas in
Unity Catalog.

questions.title: Question 20
questions.question: A window function uses the frame clause `ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW`. What does this compute?

choices.A: The aggregate over the entire partition, ignoring the current row's position
choices.B: A running (cumulative) aggregate from the first row of the partition to the current row
choices.C: The aggregate over a 30-day rolling window
choices.D: The aggregate between the first and last rows of the entire result set

questions.shortAnswer:
`ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW` defines a frame that starts at the
first row of the partition and ends at the current row. Combined with `SUM` or `COUNT`,
this produces a running (cumulative) total. `UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING`
would span the entire partition.

questions.title: Question 21
questions.question: `NTILE(4) OVER (ORDER BY revenue DESC)` — what does this function do?

choices.A: Returns the top 4 rows by revenue
choices.B: Divides the result set into 4 equal-sized buckets and assigns each row a bucket number (1–4)
choices.C: Returns the revenue values at the 25th, 50th, 75th, and 100th percentiles
choices.D: Counts the number of distinct revenue values in each quartile

questions.shortAnswer:
`NTILE(n)` divides the ordered result set into `n` equal-sized buckets and assigns each row
a bucket number from 1 to n. With `NTILE(4)` ordered by `revenue DESC`, bucket 1 contains
the top quartile earners and bucket 4 contains the bottom quartile. Rows are distributed as
evenly as possible across buckets.

questions.title: Question 22
questions.question: What does `LPAD('42', 6, '0')` return?
questions.shortAnswer:
`LPAD(string, target_length, pad_character)` left-pads the string to the target length using
the pad character. `LPAD('42', 6, '0')` produces `'000042'` — four zeros prepended to make
the total length 6. `RPAD` would pad on the right instead.

questions.title: Question 23
questions.question: Which operator tests for equality between two values including the case where both are NULL?

choices.B: `IS NOT DISTINCT FROM`
choices.D: Both B and C are correct

questions.shortAnswer:
Standard `=` returns NULL (not TRUE) when either operand is NULL, so `NULL = NULL` evaluates
to NULL (not TRUE). Both `<=>` (null-safe equality, Spark SQL) and `IS NOT DISTINCT FROM`
(standard SQL) return TRUE when both sides are NULL. In Databricks SQL, both syntaxes are
supported.

questions.title: Question 24
questions.question: What is the difference between `COALESCE(a, b, c)` and `IFNULL(a, b)`?

choices.A: They are identical — `IFNULL` is just an alias for `COALESCE`
choices.B: `COALESCE` accepts two or more arguments and returns the first non-NULL; `IFNULL` accepts exactly two arguments and returns the second if the first is NULL
choices.C: `IFNULL` is for numeric types only; `COALESCE` works on all types
choices.D: `COALESCE` short-circuits evaluation; `IFNULL` always evaluates all arguments

questions.shortAnswer:
`COALESCE(a, b, c, ...)` accepts two or more arguments and returns the first non-NULL value
in the list. `IFNULL(a, b)` is a two-argument shorthand that returns `b` when `a` is NULL.
`IFNULL(a, b)` is functionally equivalent to `COALESCE(a, b)`. `NVL` (Oracle-derived) is
another synonym for the two-argument form, also supported in Databricks SQL.

questions.title: Question 25
questions.question: What does `SPLIT('hello,world,foo', ',')` return?

choices.A: The number of commas in the string
choices.B: An array: `['hello', 'world', 'foo']`
choices.C: A single string with commas removed: `'helloworldfoo'`
choices.D: Three separate rows: `'hello'`, `'world'`, `'foo'`

questions.shortAnswer:
`SPLIT(string, delimiter)` returns an array of strings split by the delimiter. To convert the
array into separate rows, use `EXPLODE(SPLIT(...))`. `SPLIT` alone returns a single array
value in one row.

questions.title: Question 26
questions.question: `DATE_TRUNC('month', '2025-07-15')` — what does this return?

choices.A: `'2025-07-15'` — no change for a date value
choices.B: `'2025-07-01'` — truncates to the first day of the month
choices.C: `'2025-01-01'` — truncates to the first month of the year
choices.D: `'2025-07-31'` — rounds to the last day of the month

questions.shortAnswer:
`DATE_TRUNC(unit, date)` truncates a date or timestamp to the specified unit. Truncating to
`'month'` sets the day to 1 and (for timestamps) clears time components. The result is
`2025-07-01`. Common units: `'year'`, `'quarter'`, `'month'`, `'week'`, `'day'`, `'hour'`.

questions.title: Question 27
questions.question: An analyst needs to count orders where `status = 'completed'` within a larger aggregation query. Which SQL expression achieves this without a subquery?

choices.A: `COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE status = 'completed')`
choices.B: `SUM(CASE WHEN status = 'completed' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)`
choices.C: Both A and B are correct
choices.D: `COUNT_IF(status = 'completed')`

questions.shortAnswer:
All three approaches produce the same result. `SUM(CASE WHEN ...)` is the most portable
across SQL dialects. `FILTER (WHERE ...)` is ANSI SQL standard and supported in Databricks
SQL. `COUNT_IF(condition)` is a Databricks-specific shorthand. All avoid subqueries and
work inline with other aggregations in the same `SELECT`.

questions.title: Question 28
questions.question: An analyst uses a `CROSS JOIN` between a `products` table (500 rows) and a `dates` table (365 rows) to generate all product-date combinations. How many rows does the result contain?

choices.C: 500 rows — the larger table determines row count
choices.D: 365 rows — the smaller table determines row count

questions.shortAnswer:
A `CROSS JOIN` produces the Cartesian product of both tables: every row in `products` paired
with every row in `dates`. 500 × 365 = 182,500 rows. Generating all product-date combinations
for a sales grid or template table is a classic valid use case for `CROSS JOIN`.

questions.title: Question 29
questions.question: A query uses `WHERE product_id NOT IN (SELECT product_id FROM returns)`. The `returns` table contains one NULL in `product_id`. What result does the main query return?

choices.A: All rows where `product_id` is not in the non-NULL values of `returns`
choices.B: Zero rows — `NOT IN` with a subquery containing NULL always returns an empty result
choices.C: An error — NULL values are not permitted in `NOT IN` subqueries
choices.D: The same result as using `NOT EXISTS`

questions.shortAnswer:
This is a classic SQL null-trap. `NOT IN` evaluates as `value != x AND value != y AND value != NULL`.
Since any comparison with NULL returns UNKNOWN (not TRUE), the entire `NOT IN` condition
becomes UNKNOWN for every row — and only TRUE rows pass the WHERE filter. The result is zero
rows. Use `NOT EXISTS` or filter NULLs from the subquery to avoid this behavior.

questions.title: Question 30
questions.question: What does the `INTERSECT` set operator return?

choices.A: All rows from both queries combined, with duplicates removed
choices.B: Only rows that appear in both query result sets
choices.C: Rows from the first query that do not appear in the second query
choices.D: All rows from both queries, including duplicates

questions.shortAnswer:
`INTERSECT` returns only rows that appear in both query results (the set intersection).
`UNION` combines all rows (removing duplicates), `UNION ALL` combines with duplicates,
and `EXCEPT` (or `MINUS`) returns rows in the first set not in the second.

questions.title: Question 31
questions.question: What does the `EXCEPT` set operator return?

choices.A: Rows that appear in either the first or second query, but not both
choices.B: Rows from the first query result that do not appear in the second query result
choices.C: Rows from the second query result that do not appear in the first query result
choices.D: The symmetric difference of both result sets

questions.shortAnswer:
`EXCEPT` returns rows present in the first query's result but absent from the second query's
result. It removes duplicates by default (use `EXCEPT ALL` to retain them). This is equivalent
to a `LEFT ANTI JOIN` on the full set of columns.

questions.title: Question 32
questions.question: An analyst writes: `SELECT region, revenue, DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY revenue DESC) AS dr FROM sales QUALIFY dr <= 3`. What does this return?

choices.A: The top 3 revenue rows only
choices.B: All rows where the dense rank of revenue is 1, 2, or 3 — multiple rows per rank if tied
choices.C: An error — `QUALIFY` cannot be used with `DENSE_RANK()`
choices.D: The top 3 distinct revenue values with one row each

questions.shortAnswer:
`DENSE_RANK()` assigns the same rank to tied rows and never skips rank numbers. `QUALIFY dr <= 3`
keeps all rows where the dense rank is 1, 2, or 3. If three rows share rank 1, all three are
returned. This pattern is useful for returning the top-N ranked groups including all ties.

questions.title: Question 33
questions.question: A Databricks SQL dashboard contains a Text widget with markdown content. What is the primary use case for a Text widget?

choices.A: Displaying a live query result in formatted text
choices.B: Adding static documentation, instructions, section headings, or context to a dashboard
choices.C: Showing the last refresh timestamp automatically
choices.D: Embedding an external URL in the dashboard

questions.shortAnswer:
Text widgets render static markdown content — headings, explanatory text, instructions, or
links. They are used to add context and documentation to dashboards. Text widgets do not
execute queries or display live data. For a live single-metric display, use a Counter widget.

questions.title: Question 34
questions.question: A dashboard has four permission levels. Which level allows a user to run queries and see updated results, but not change query SQL or dashboard layout?

choices.A: Can View
choices.B: Can Run
choices.C: Can Edit
choices.D: Owner

questions.shortAnswer:
"Can Run" allows a user to trigger a dashboard refresh (re-execute underlying queries) and
view the updated results. "Can View" shows the last cached results only — no refresh capability.
"Can Edit" allows modifying queries and layout. "Owner" has full control including deletion.

questions.title: Question 35
questions.question: A single SQL query returns columns for `region`, `product`, and `revenue`. An analyst wants both a bar chart and a table visualization from this same query result. What should they do?

choices.A: Run the query twice and create one visualization from each run
choices.B: Add multiple visualizations to the same query — Databricks SQL allows multiple visualizations per query result
choices.C: Create two separate queries with identical SQL
choices.D: Use a UNION to combine the data for the chart and the table

questions.shortAnswer:
In Databricks SQL, a single query can have multiple visualizations attached to it. Each
visualization (bar chart, table, pie chart, counter, etc.) draws from the same query result.
When the query refreshes, all visualizations update together. This avoids duplicating query
logic.

questions.title: Question 36
questions.question: Which export options are available from a Databricks SQL dashboard visualization?

choices.A: PDF of the full dashboard page
choices.B: CSV download of the underlying query result for individual visualizations
choices.C: Excel (.xlsx) format only
choices.D: Both A and B are available

questions.shortAnswer:
Databricks SQL supports downloading individual visualization data as CSV (from the
visualization's kebab menu) and downloading a PDF snapshot of the full dashboard. This
enables analysts to share static snapshots or extract data for external use.

questions.title: Question 37
questions.question: When configuring a Databricks SQL alert, which field allows customizing the email subject line?

choices.A: Alerts do not support custom email subjects — a system-generated subject is always used
choices.B: The "Custom subject" field in the alert notification settings
choices.C: The alert name automatically becomes the email subject
choices.D: Email subjects can only be customized via a webhook integration

questions.shortAnswer:
When configuring an alert's notification destinations, Databricks SQL provides a "Custom
subject" field for email notifications. This allows the alert creator to specify a descriptive
subject line such as "ALERT: Revenue dropped below threshold" instead of the default
system-generated subject.

questions.title: Question 38
questions.question: A dashboard is scheduled to refresh daily using Warehouse A. Warehouse A is then deleted by an admin. What happens to the scheduled refresh?

choices.A: The refresh fails and the dashboard owner receives an error notification
choices.B: The refresh automatically switches to the default warehouse
choices.C: The refresh runs on any available warehouse in the workspace
choices.D: The refresh silently fails with no notification until manually reconfigured

questions.shortAnswer:
The scheduled refresh fails because the assigned warehouse no longer exists. The dashboard
owner receives a failure notification. The refresh does not automatically migrate to another
warehouse — an admin or owner must update the dashboard's refresh schedule to select a
new warehouse.

questions.title: Question 39
questions.question: An analyst creates a bar chart and wants the bars for `revenue > 1,000,000` to appear in green and all others in gray. How is this achieved?

choices.A: This conditional coloring is not supported — all bars use a single color
choices.B: Use a `CASE WHEN` in SQL to add a category column, then use that column as the "Color" or "Group By" dimension in the chart
choices.C: Write a Python script to post-process the chart colors
choices.D: Use the chart's "Threshold" option to set color ranges

questions.shortAnswer:
To achieve conditional coloring, add a computed category column in SQL:
`CASE WHEN revenue > 1000000 THEN 'High' ELSE 'Normal' END AS revenue_tier`.
Set the chart's "Color" or "Group By" to `revenue_tier` and assign colors per category
in the chart configuration. This is the standard pattern for conditional visualization
styling in Databricks SQL.

questions.title: Question 40
questions.question: A team wants to schedule one dashboard to refresh every hour on a high-capacity warehouse during business hours and use a smaller warehouse for the nightly refresh to save cost. How can this be achieved?

choices.A: This is not possible — a dashboard can only have one schedule and one assigned warehouse
choices.B: Create two separate schedule entries on the dashboard, each with a different time window and a different assigned warehouse
choices.C: Use a Databricks Job with two tasks to swap the warehouse assignment before each run
choices.D: Configure warehouse auto-scaling to switch sizes based on time of day

questions.shortAnswer:
Databricks SQL dashboard scheduling supports only one active refresh schedule per dashboard,
with a single assigned warehouse. To use different warehouses at different times, you would
need to manage this through the Databricks REST API or Jobs — not through the dashboard
scheduling UI directly. Awareness of this limitation is important for exam scenarios
involving cost optimization and scheduling.

questions.title: Question 41
questions.question: A dashboard filter needs to let users pick a date range (start date and end date). Does Databricks SQL support a native date range parameter type?

choices.A: No — date range must be implemented as two separate text parameters
choices.B: Yes — Databricks SQL supports a Date Range parameter type that provides a calendar picker with start and end date selection
choices.C: Yes — but only for Serverless SQL warehouses
choices.D: No — date ranges require a custom dropdown with hard-coded values

questions.shortAnswer:
Databricks SQL supports several parameter types including Text, Number, Dropdown List, Query
Dropdown, Date, Date and Time, and Date Range. The Date Range type renders a calendar picker
with start and end date selection and injects both values into the query using the
`{{parameter_name.start}}` and `{{parameter_name.end}}` syntax.

questions.title: Question 42
questions.question: A query parameter `{{min_revenue}}` is used in a dashboard filter. The analyst wants new users to see data filtered to `100000` until they change the filter. How is this configured?

choices.A: Default values cannot be set — users must always select a value before the query runs
choices.B: Set a default value for the parameter in the query settings — this value is used when no user selection has been made
choices.C: Hard-code `100000` in the SQL and add `{{min_revenue}}` as a comment
choices.D: Use `COALESCE({{min_revenue}}, 100000)` in the SQL

questions.shortAnswer:
Databricks SQL query parameters support default values configured in the query or dashboard
parameter settings. When a user opens the dashboard without having selected a value, the
default is used, ensuring the query runs immediately with sensible initial data rather than
failing or showing no filter.

questions.title: Question 43
questions.question: An analyst shares a saved query with a colleague using "Can View" access. What can the colleague do with the query?

choices.A: Edit the SQL and save changes
choices.B: Run the query and view results, but not modify the SQL or save changes
choices.C: Share the query further with additional users
choices.D: Delete the query

questions.shortAnswer:
"Can View" on a saved query allows the recipient to open and read the SQL, run the query,
and view results. They cannot modify the SQL, rename, delete, or share the query further.
"Can Edit" permission is required to make and save changes to the query.

questions.title: Question 44
questions.question: A SQL alert monitors a query that returns no rows (an empty result). The alert condition is `WHEN VALUE > 0`. Does the alert fire?

choices.A: Yes — an empty result is treated as 0, which is not greater than 0, so no alert fires
choices.B: No — when the query returns no rows, the alert condition evaluates to UNKNOWN and does not fire
choices.C: Yes — an empty result always triggers an alert as an error condition
choices.D: The alert raises an error and sends an error notification

questions.shortAnswer:
When a monitored query returns no rows, the alert value is effectively undefined (NULL).
Databricks SQL treats a NULL result as UNKNOWN, which does not satisfy the threshold
condition. The alert does not fire. If detecting the absence of data (zero rows) is the
goal, rewrite the query to return a `COUNT(*)` which will return 0 instead of no rows.

questions.title: Question 45
questions.question: Which approach is the documented and safest fit?

choices.A: Standard view: `CREATE VIEW gold.daily_revenue AS SELECT date, SUM(amount) ... GROUP BY date`
choices.B: Materialized view: `CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW gold.daily_revenue AS ...` with an hourly refresh schedule
choices.C: Pin the dashboard to a Pro SQL Warehouse with result caching enabled
choices.D: Convert the orders table to bucketing on date

questions.shortAnswer: Genie Spaces is the documented natural-language analytics surface for exactly this use case. UC permissions, including column masks and row filters, apply to every Genie-generated SQL query — so CS users physically cannot see PII columns they're not granted on, regardless of how Genie phrases the question. Option A reinvents Genie poorly. Option C duplicates data and creates a refresh / drift problem. Option D is a retrieval-augmentation pattern over *unstructured* data — vector search returns similar rows by embedding distance, which is the wrong tool for structured analytic queries (counts, aggregates, joins).
